coredata.dc:description: Nitrite is limited to apply in meat products as colorant, which probably leads to food poisoning and increased cancer risk when excessively consumed. Consequently, the development of simple and sensitive methods for the detection of nitrite is indispensable. N, P and S co-doped carbon dots emitting red fluorescence (R-CDs) are prepared by the one-step hydrothermal strategy with o-phenylenediamine, p-aminobenzoic acid, sulfuric acid and phosphoric acid as precursors. The optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of R-CDs are 567 nm and 632 nm (the fluorescence quantum yield of 0.23), respectively. And the prepared R-CDs have satisfactory photostability at room temperature. The R-CDs are in possession of high selectivity for nitrite with the response time of 5 min that could be applied to fluorescence and ratiometric absorption spectra dual-mode detection for nitrite with the fluorescence detection range of 5.00–67.50 µM and the detection limit (LOD) of 0.89 µM. The fluorescence of R-CDs at 632 nm could be statically quenched by NO2 –. Subsequently, the detection range of ratio absorption method is 10.00–180.00 µM whose correlation coefficient is 0.9999 and the LOD is calculated to be 0.27 µM. Ultimately, the R-CDs-based smart phone and strip sensing methods are established to achieve the simple detection for nitrite. The constructed fluorescence and absorption spectroscopy-based sensing methods have expansive application prospect as a rapid and improvised strategy for the detection of nitrite in daily life.

head.ce:title: N, P and S co-doped red fluorescent carbon dots sensing for nitrite by fluorescent/colorimetric/smart phone/test strip methods through diazo coupling reaction in food samples

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, and Institute of Environmental Science, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, and Institute of Environmental Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, and Institute of Environmental Science, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrite is limited to apply in meat products as colorant, which probably leads to food poisoning and increased cancer risk when excessively consumed. Consequently, the development of simple and sensitive methods for the detection of nitrite is indispensable. N, P and S co-doped carbon dots emitting red fluorescence (R-CDs) are prepared by the one-step hydrothermal strategy with o-phenylenediamine, p-aminobenzoic acid, sulfuric acid and phosphoric acid as precursors. The optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of R-CDs are 567 nm and 632 nm (the fluorescence quantum yield of 0.23), respectively. And the prepared R-CDs have satisfactory photostability at room temperature. The R-CDs are in possession of high selectivity for nitrite with the response time of 5 min that could be applied to fluorescence and ratiometric absorption spectra dual-mode detection for nitrite with the fluorescence detection range of 5.00–67.50 µM and the detection limit (LOD) of 0.89 µM. The fluorescence of R-CDs at 632 nm could be statically quenched by NO2 –. Subsequently, the detection range of ratio absorption method is 10.00–180.00 µM whose correlation coefficient is 0.9999 and the LOD is calculated to be 0.27 µM. Ultimately, the R-CDs-based smart phone and strip sensing methods are established to achieve the simple detection for nitrite. The constructed fluorescence and absorption spectroscopy-based sensing methods have expansive application prospect as a rapid and improvised strategy for the detection of nitrite in daily life.

ce:keyword.ce:text: N,P,S co-doped red fluorescence carbon dots

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nitrite is quantity limited when applied in meat products as colorant, which probably causes food poisoning [1–3]. Ingestion of 0.3–0.5 g of nitrite can cause poisoning, and 3.0 g may further lead to death. Nitrates or nitrites ingested under the endogenous nitrosation conditions have been listed in category 2A carcinogens by the World Health Organization's (WHO) International Agency for Research on Cancer (IARC) on October 27, 2017. The average exposures over time of nitrite in the drinking-water should not exceed about 0.2 mg∙L-1 with the usage of intermittent chloramine [4]. It is established by U.S Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) that the nitrite content in the distribution system from tap is limited as ex water treatment works of 0.50 mg∙L-1 while final water after treatment of 0.10 mg∙L-1 [5], correspondingly. Therefore, the construct of simple and sensitive methods for nitrite detection is necessary.

Up to now, strategy of trace nitrite detection mainly includes electrochemical sensor [6–8], portable gas pressure equipment [9], spectroscopy [1–3,10–15] and chromatography [16], etc. Electrochemical materials [7] (for instance, polymers, oxides and hydroxides, etc.) arouse widespread attention for their excellent sensitivity, low limit of detection (LOD), and rapid response capability, while their popularization is confined by the inherent defects of chemically modified electrodes with poor stability and reproducibility. The gas pressure and chromatography strategy seem not appropriate to be utilized for their complex synthesis steps and expensive detection costs, without regard to their significant anti-interference and wide linear range. Spectroscopic methods (like spectrophotometry [2,10–12,14], fluorescence spectroscopy [1,11,13] and Raman spectroscopy [3,14], etc.) have increasingly become research hotspots about instant and convenient detection of food additives for the advantages of on-site naked-eye detection, real-time tracking in organisms and intelligent portable equipment. As the traditional nitrite colorimetric sensors, Griess reagents could react with the nitrite to form the diazo compounds under acidic conditions, which further couple with naphthalene vinyldiamine, and the red product concentration generated with a linear relationship [14] between their absorbances and nitrite concentration (the maximum absorption peak at 540 nm). However, the ratio absorption spectral methods could accurately detect trace components, especially in complex matrices, with the sensitivity effectively improved by measuring the absorption changes of the sample at different wavelengths. Novel fluorescent materials [17–20] like organic molecule probes [13,21], metal nanoclusters [22] and inorganic quantum dots have been prevailingly employed in fluorescence spectroscopy [12,15,23] to determine the concentration of nitrite by diazotization [24], nitrification, and coordination reaction mechanism [25]. However, the preparation of small molecule fluorescent probes seems complicate. Metal nanoclusters and inorganic quantum dots have remarkable resistance to photobleaching with the inevitable biological toxicity.

Carbon dots (CDs) have potential applications in biomedical, sensors, optoelectronic equipment and other related fields thanks to their tunable optical properties, eminent biocompatibility and moderate reaction conditions. The abundant surface functional groups on CDs not only increase the solubility of CDs in polar solvents, but also make it possible to detect various small molecules (such as metal ions [26–29], anions [30,31], antibiotics [32–34] and amino acids [35]). Karali et al. have synthesized blue fluorescent CDs for nitrite detection based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) selecting urea and tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane as precursors in 2018. It exhibits the distinguished linear relation within nitrite concentration of 72.00–600.00 μM and the LOD of 13.50 μM [23]. Although the probe shows satisfactory recovery and anti-interference quality, the emission wavelengths of the probes at the blue light region still limit the applications of CDs in biomedicine due to the defect of susceptibility to interference by biological autofluorescence. Guo et al. have designed a handheld sensing platform (HSP) for the qualitative and quantitative detection of nitrite in 2024 [12]. In this project, the orange-yellow fluorescent CDs are prepared and the optical signals are subsequently converted into HSP voltage by photoelectric conversion technology with the results of linear range from 10.00 μM to 500.00 μM and LOD of 1.95 μM. Not only the design of long-wavelength emission CDs to achieve high selectivity and low LOD for NO2 –, but also their proposal of convenient visual probe strategy is continuously the focus issue for academic circles now [8,17–20,29].

According to the optimization experiments of R-CDs synthesis conditions (Fig. S1), the quantum yields of prepared CDs (ΦS) under different conditions have been compared. The optimal experimental conditions are n (o-phenylenediamine): n (p-aminobenzoic acid) = 1.5: 1, V (H3PO4): V (H2SO4) = 1:2, 500 μL phosphoric acid, the reaction temperature of 180 °C and reaction time of 8 h. The hydrothermal synthesis of R-CDs is carried out at optimized reaction conditions, and the ΦS reaches the maximum value of 0.23 (Fig. S2).

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) has been applied to characterize the morphology and particle sizes of the prepared R-CDs. As shown in Fig. 1 A, the R-CDs are uniformly spherical dispersed, with particle size range of 9.5 nm–39.5 nm and an average particle size of 25.5 nm ± 0.2 nm. The lattice fringes have been observed by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy, and the lattice spacing is 0.21 nm, corresponding to the graphene 110 crystal plane [38], indicating that the precursors are basically completely carbonized during the hydrothermal reaction.

From the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of R-CDs (Fig. 1B–G), there are five characteristic peaks [39–43] in the result of survey scan (Fig. 1B), and the values of binding energy are O 1s (531.47 eV), N 1s (400.65 eV), C 1s (283.85 eV), S 2p3 (168.20 eV) and P 2p (133.78 eV), successively. The high-resolution XPS spectral of each element shows that the C 1s spectrum (Fig. 1C) could be analysed into 283.52 eV, 284.32 eV, 285.00 eV and 287.76 eV, which correspond to the CC, C–H/CC/C–P, C–N/C–S/C–O and OCO functional groups [39] on the surface of R-CDs, respectively. Similarly, N 1s spectral (Fig. 1D) is fitted to 399.73 eV, 400.53 eV, and 400.98 eV, i.e., pyridine nitrogen, pyrrole nitrogen, and tertiary amine nitrogen atoms directly attaching to the surface of the R-CDs [40]. The 530.86 eV, 531.60 eV, and 532.37 eV peaks in O 1s match with the oxygen-containing functional groups C–O/O–H/PO, N–O/CO/C–O [41], and S–O/P–O/C–O (Fig. 1E). Three peaks of 163.80 eV (C-S), 168.00 eV (R-SO3 2-) and 169.02 eV (SO4 2-) are obtained from the resolution of S 2p3 (Fig. 1F) [42]. For the P 2p spectrum (Fig. 1G), it can be decomposed into three characteristic peaks of 133.36 eV (P 2p3), 133.83 eV (P-C), and 134.72 eV (P-C) [43]. The XPS results indicate the successful doping of N, S and P into the R-CDs.

In order to further verify the successful doping of N, S and P, the differences on FTIR spectroscopy between o-phenylenediamine and p-aminobenzoic acid and R-CDs have been compared. As illustrated in Fig. 1H, the absorption peak at 3399 cm−1 may be attributed to the stretching vibration of O–H or N–H on the surface of R-CDs [44], and the stretching vibration of C–H at 2935 cm−1. In the same way, the characteristic peaks at 2339 cm−1 and 1626–1490 cm−1 are analyzed as P-H bending vibration and amide CO or CN stretching vibration, respectively. The strong peaks at 1121–1005 cm−1 and 748 cm−1 are attributed to the hydrogen-bonded PO, C–O or P–O–C and CS stretching vibrations [45]. At the same time, the strong carboxyl characteristic peak of p-aminobenzoic acid at 1681 cm−1 disappears basically after the hydrothermal reaction, speculated that the carboxyl groups form an amide structure with the amino group under the catalysis of sulfuric acid. In addition, the N–H out-of-plane deformation vibration peak at 900–770 cm−1 and the N–H bending vibration peak of primary amine connected to the aromatic ring skeletons at 1650–1500 cm−1 in o-phenylenediamine disappears after the formation of R-CDs. It indicates that under high temperature–pressure conditions, the strong acid environment formed by sulfuric acid and phosphoric acid provides a large number of hydrogen protons for the solute molecules, which could induce electron transfer and accelerate the reaction process. And the primary amine groups on the aromatic rings convert into heterocyclic compounds to generate larger conjugate planes for the emission of red fluorescence. The data above seems consistent with conclusions of XPS, which confirms the successful doping of N, P and S elements. And the abundant water-soluble functional groups (such as –NH2 or –COOH) on the surface of R-CDs could be used to form hydrogen bonds with highly polar solvent molecules, thereby improving the solubility of R-CDs in water.

To construct a dual-mode sensing strategy of fluorescence and UV–vis spectroscopy, the optical properties of R-CDs have been explored by UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectrometer. As for the UV–Vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 2 A), the strong absorption peak at 260 nm is attributed to the
π -
π ∗
transition of the aromatic rings or heterocyclic conjugate planes, and the shoulder peak at 276 nm is on account of the electron energy levels in conjugated rings affected by different substituents [46]. The comparatively weak peak at 410 nm corresponds to the
n -
π ∗
transitions of CO, C–O–P and C–S on R-CDs [47]. Furthermore, there is a weak absorption peak at 614 nm, which may match with the outer-shell electron transition of functional groups on the surface of R-CDs [44]. The fluorescence behaviors of R-CDs have been investigated by fluorescence spectrophotometer, that the optimal excitation wavelength (λex) locates at 567 nm with a shoulder peak at 522 nm on the left side and the maximum emission wavelength (λem) situates at 632 nm with its shoulder at 671 nm, correspondingly. It is found that the fluorescence excitation spectrum is mirror-symmetrical with the emission spectrum where the Stokes shift is 65 nm, which could avoid the interference of excitation light and stray light to the detection system. As revealed in Fig. 2B, the R-CDs equip with excitation independence that the maximum emission intensity increases first and decreases soon afterwards with the excitation wavelengths from 507 nm to 597 nm, that the maximum and minimum emission intensity is 940 a.u. and 145 a.u., respectively, while the λem remains basically unchanged [44]. The CIE 1931 (Standard Colorimetric System) coordinates are calculated to inquire the color of R-CDs under 567 nm excitation light and daylight, and found that the fluorescence (Fig. 2C) of R-CDs is located at (0.699, 0.301) and the color (Fig. 2D) coordinate under daylight is (0.282, 0.250). Consequences above confirm that R-CDs exhibit red fluorescence, which provides the potential for biological cell imaging because of the merit free from disturbing by short-wavelength fluorescence during detection. The quantum yield ΦS of R-CDs calculated by the five-point method is 0.23 with the cresyl violet acetate (Φ0 = 0.54) as reference (Fig. S2).

In order to judge the accuracy and precision of targeted detection by R-CDs, the photostability of R-CDs in diverse environments has been explored. Firstly, R-CDs have been introduced into NaCl aqueous solutions with high ionic strength (0.25–2.00 M) to determine the fluorescence intensity at 632 nm. Here, the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs in deionized water is established as F0, the relative fluorescence intensity of F/F0 fluctuates slightly with the increase of NaCl concentration (Fig. S3A), that the maximum and minimum relative intensity is 1.03 and 0.87, respectively, indicating the distinguished salt stability of R-CDs.

Secondly, a line chart of F/F0 against time is made, where the fluorescence intensity under the irradiation of Xenon arc lamp or UV lamp for a period of time is F while the initial fluorescence intensity of R-CDs is recorded as F0 (Fig. S3B). The results manifest that the relative fluorescence intensity of F/F0 remains nearly constant within 60 min, and the experimental error of trice repeated measurements is no more than 4 %. The fluorescence intensity is still maintained under the continuous excitation, demonstrating that the light radiation could barely change the configuration of the luminescent particles or trigger chemical reactions, that is to say, R-CDs have eminent resistance to photobleaching.

Eventually, R-CDs have been added to the phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH = 1.00–14.00), which shows irregular fluorescence behaviors (Fig. S3C). In the strongly acidic environment, the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs enhances significantly, compared with that in deionized water. While the fluorescence is almost completely quenched when PBS (pH = 11.00–12.00) is applied as the solvent, with the color transformation of the solutions from olive drab to orange under sunlight. It may have undergone the protonation-deprotonation process that the plentiful protons in the solution might form intermolecular or intramolecular hydrogen bonds with luminescent particles (R-CDs) under strong acidic conditions, resulting in the expansion of the rigid planes with the enhancement of fluorescence intensity and the red-shift of the optimal emission wavelength. Inversely, the fractional protons used to form hydrogen bonds are consumed in alkaline solutions, and the aromatic or heterocyclic rings therewith undergo the conformational conversion through the rotation of covalent bonds to quench fluorescence [44]. What’ s more, fluorescence intensity of R-CDs slightly increases when the pH changes from 3.00 to 5.00 while mildly decreases in the pH range from 5.00 to 11.00. Within the pH range of 4.00–5.00, the fluorescence intensity is strong and relatively stable. And R-CDs could also exhibit strong fluorescence intensity in ultrapure water.

As shown in Fig. 3 , nitrite and dichromate could remarkably quench the fluorescence of R-CDs, declaring that R-CDs unfold favorable selectivity for nitrate and dichromate. Nevertheless, there is little dichromate in food for that the oxidation ability of dichromate is stronger than that of nitrite, meaning that the nitrite can seldom coexist with dichromate. Therefore, the prepared R-CDs possess the capability to detect nitrite. Subsequently, the accuracy of the quantitative analysis of NO2 – by R-CDs with the 10-fold concentrations of interferences coexisted has been deliberated as follow. The fluorescence intensity in existence of high concentrations of Zn2+ or Hg2+ diminishes with flocculent precipitation produced, which may be ascribed to the coordination of metal ions with functional groups on the surface of R-CDs. Therefore, the tolerance to Zn2+ or Hg2+ is 7 times or 8 times of measured NO2 –, respectively. Further analysis of the disturbance of S2O3 2-, SCN- and I- alleges that the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs declines when these three interferences are introduced, and the fluorescence is quenched incompletely after the addition of NO2 –. Concerning about nitrite, there is the dynamic equilibrium of HNO2(aq) ⇌ H+(aq) + NO2 –(aq), where nitrous acid (HNO2) is a moderately weak acid (pKa = 3.35) while nitrite is a fairly strong base that tend to combine with hydrogen ions. According to the Nernst’s equation and the chemical equilibrium equation, the oxidation of nitrite significantly enhances therewith when the pH decreases. The proportion and reduction potential of nitrite in the solution where the pH is less than 3.35 is counted below 50 % and approximate to 1.00 V, which has strong oxidation for iodine ions under acidity. Analogously, all three anions have reducibility whose standard electrode potentials are negative. The reaction between NO2 – and R-CDs might compete with the three anionics interferences, where NO2 – preferentially reacts with reacts with the functional groups on the surface of R-CDs, so that the concentration of NO2 – responsible for fluorescence quenching has reduced, making the reduction of the fluorescence quenching degree of R-CDs. 10 times the concentration of acetate ions can also significantly reduce the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs, therefore the tolerance of this detection system for acetate ions is 6 times the concentration of NO2 –. In summary, R-CDs displays good selectivity and anti-interference to probe NO2 – specifically.

Determination conditions of R-CDs for NO2 – have been optimized for the improvement on the accurate measurement. Three parameters have been selected, the optimum detection concentration of R-CDs, the response time and the most suitable pH for determination. 300.00 mg∙mL−1 of R-CDs stock solution has been primarily added to 2 mL of deionized water sequentially, and it could be found that the fluorescence intensity increases from 128 a.u. to 796 a.u. with the addition of R-CDs concentration in the range of 0.30 mg∙mL−1 to 4.05 mg∙mL−1 (as depicted in the fluorescence titration curve), whose tangential slope of the curve (Fig. S4A inset) decreases thereupon with the emission wavelength slight redshift. After the bulk, volume of R-CDs stock solution equivalent to 26 μL, 1 μL is added at a time until the fluorescence intensity decreased by self-quenching. 4.20 mg∙mL−1 is ascertained as the determination concentration in consequence.

Secondly, 13.5 μL of 0.01 M NaNO2 (total concentration of 67.50 μM) is added dropwise to R-CDs solution of 4.2 mg∙mL−1, and the fluorescence intensity is therewith observed after fixing the excitation and emission wavelength [48–50]. As illustrated in Fig. S4B, the fluorescence intensity descends over time and begins to level off after 5 min, where the incubation time of R-CDs for the detection of NO2 – is established to be 5 min. Ultimately, the detection sensitivity in different pH environments (PBS) has been compared. The difference in fluorescence intensity before and after the addition of NO2 – (F-F0) has been calculated, and the maximum, i.e., pH = 4.00 in PBS, has confirmed for subsequent experiments (Fig. S3C). Secondly, 13.5 μL of 0.01 M NaNO2 (total concentration of 67.50 μM) is added dropwise to R-CDs solution of 4.2 mg∙mL−1, and the fluorescence intensity is therewith observed after fixing the excitation and emission wavelength. As illustrated in Fig. S4B, the fluorescence intensity descends over time and begins to level off after 5 min, where the incubation time of R-CDs for the detection of NO2 – is established to be 5 min. Ultimately, the detection sensitivity in different pH environments (PBS) has been compared. The difference in fluorescence intensity before and after the addition of NO2 – (F-F0) has been calculated, and the maximum, i.e., pH = 4.00 in PBS, has confirmed for subsequent experiments (Fig. S3C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of NO2 – by R-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To establish an effective NO2 – analysis method, it is essential to evaluate the quantitative accuracy, detection of limit (LOD) and detection range of R-CDs for NO2 –. As exhibited in the diagram (Fig. 4 A), series NaNO2 (2 × 10-3 M) with different volumes have been introduced into the solution containing 4.2 mg∙mL−1 R-CDs at optimal pH 4.00, and the ultimate concentrations of NaNO2 are 0.0, 2.5, 5.0, 7.5, 10.0, 12.5, 15.0, 17.5, … μM, respectively. The fluorescence intensity decreases with the increase of NO2 – concentration when the concentration of NaNO2 situates in the interval of 5.00–47.50 μM. While in the range of 47.50–67.50 μM μM, the fluorescence quenching rate accelerates until the fluorescence is completely quenched. With the blue shift of emission wavelengths, the fluorescence transition from red to green can be observed with naked eyes, which indicates that the NaNO2 may be detected visually.

The maximum emission intensity before and after the addition of NaNO2 is denoted as F0 and F, respectively, and the relative fluorescence intensity (F0-F)/F0 is calculated. As Fig. 4B of (F0-F)/F0 with the concentration of NaNO2, it is exponentially fitted as y = 9.0480∙exp(x/10.0411)-0.0179 (n = 21, R2 = 0.9970) in the determination range of 5.00–67.50 μL. The linear relationship in 5.00–47.50 μL is calculated as y = 0.0081x-0.1217 (n = 13, R2 = 0.9646) with LOD (LOD = 3σ/S, Where, σ is the standard deviation of instrument noise after at least 20 measurements of blank samples, and S is the slope of the working curve.) of 0.89 μM. Compared with the literatures on NO2 – detection that have been reported, the established analytical method has the lower LOD and broader detection range, which could be proposed for the quantitative detection of nitrite in actual samples. The comparation of the detection methods with those reported in other literatures have been listed in Table S2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection mechanism of NO2 – by R-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: As analyzed in Fig. 4B, the fitting curve slopes of the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs with NO2 – concentration increase considerably. The mechanism of the fluorescent detection of NO2 – by R-CDs has been further explored. From the UV–Vis absorption spectrum in Fig. 4C, the single absorption peak of NO2 – appears from 265 to 400 nm with the maximum absorption wavelength at 356 nm. The fluorescent excitation spectrum of the prepared R-CDs is yet from 425 nm onwards. There are scarcely overlap between the absorption spectrum of NO2 – and the excitation spectrum of R-CDs indicating that internal filtering effect does not exist.

To comprehend the fluorescent emission behaviors of R-CDs before and after the instillation of NO2 – (0.00, 10.00, 20.00, 30.00 and 40.00 μM of concentrations), the transient-steady fluorescence spectrometer has been utilized to compare the attenuation curves of the luminescent particles (Fig. 4D), and the fluorescence lifetimes are calculated by tail fitting (Table S1). The fluorescence lifetime of R-CDs is 1.4995 ns, and fluorescence lifetimes of R-CDs with NO2 – prove approximately 1.459–1.599 ns at room temperature. There is no apparent lifetime change implying that the dynamic quenching mechanism could be excluded.

The Zeta potential Fig. 4E has therewith estimated that the initial surface potential R-CDs is negative (−1.45 mV) and that of NaNO2 has been measured as 6.57 mV. Then the potential increased significantly from −0.05, 0.02 to 0.17 eV with the analyte concentration grown from 20, 30 to 40 µM). The absolute value of the Zeta potential represents the stability of the colloidal solution. That is to say, because of the high specific surface area and surface energy of nanoparticles (R-CDs), the nitrite ions with positive charge are prone to approach R-CDs by electrostatic interaction to reduce the surface energy for the stability of the reaction system. Subsequently, under the acidic condition (pH = 4), the diazo coupling reaction of the two occurs, which reduces the negative charge on the surface of R-CDs and the apparent specific surface energy of the R-CDs solution. At the same time, a new substance (diazoamine compounds) speculates to be produced through the reaction of NO2 – with R-CDs, which underlies the colorimetry detection of NaNO2.

Secondly, the UV–Vis absorption spectra of the mixed system before and after the addition of NO2 – (Fig. 5 A) depicts that the absorbance at 320–380 nm climbs with a new peak appearing at 350 nm. The peak at 410 nm slides meanwhile with an isoabsorption point appeared at 388 nm, where the solution colour turns from grass green to yellow. A new substance speculates to be produced through the reaction of NO2 – with R-CDs, which underlies the colorimetry detection of NaNO2.

Thirdly, the Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy of R-CDs, NaNO2 and their reaction products (Fig. S5) have been compared that the prepared R-CDs are rich in primary ammonia and carboxyl groups, and the absorption peaks in the range of 3500–3200 cm−1 are judged as the expansion vibrations of –OH and –NH2. While –NH3 + is formed for the strong acidity of as-prepared R-CDs, which prompts the absorption peaks of primary amines move to the low frequency region of 2800 cm−1. The 3200–2500 cm−1 interval centered at 3000 cm−1 is homologous to the –OH stretching vibration of the carboxylic acid dimer. The absorption peak of primary amines in the range of 3200–2500 cm−1 significantly declines, compared with the spectroscopy of R-CDs and R-CDs with NO2 –. It asserts that the content of primary amines has accordingly dropped. Nevertheless, the absorption of carboxyldimers exists, and the characteristic wide peak of 950 cm−1 matches to the OH∙∙∙O, which could be confirmed as the carboxyl group. Except for this, the drop of N-O absorption peak at 1264 cm−1 signs the decrease of nitro group of NO2 –.

The quenching constant declines as the temperature rises for static quenching, which is verified converse for dynamic quenching. The fluorescence titration experiments have been performed at 30 °C and 45 °C, respectively. The relative fluorescence intensity F0/F have been plotted with the concentrations of NO2 – at the 632 nm fluorescence emission peak (Fig. 4F). On the one hand, when the temperature ascends from 30 °C to 45 °C, the Stern-Volmer equation correlates linearly in the NO2 – concentration range from 0.00 μM to 40.00 μM. And the phenomena that the slope drops with the jump of temperature [51–53] attest the fluorescent static quench means. On the other hand, the upward tendency of the Stern-Volmer curve when the concentration of NO2 – is greater than 50.00 μM declares the accelerated quenching velocity. And the incremental probability of collision by the attraction between the particles implicates the dynamic quenching mechanism, consisted with the results in Fig. 4E. In summary, the response of R-CDs to nitrite at low concentrations is dominated by static quenching. When the concentration of NO2 – grows, the collision quenching comes staple with the continuous increase of particles quantity in the solution, where the dynamic and static corporate quenching presents.

The titration experiment is carried out at pH = 4.00 in PBS, where the amount of feeblish acid afforded seems insufficient to 100 µmol of acid. Taking the plentiful primary amino groups on the surface of R-CDs into consideration, the titration reaction may produce diazoamine compounds through the reaction mechanism shown in Fig. 4G. Even if there is relatively strong acidic at the beginning of titration to form diazonium salt cations, they still tend to couple and form diazoamine compounds when the affluent primary amino groups surrounded, owing to their weakness of electrophile.

The content of determination of nitrite in this study may not be accurately measured in neutral/alkaline and strongly oxidizing/reducing environments. Because the diazotization reaction cannot be completed in neutral/alkaline environments, the measurement cannot be completed. If there are oxidizing substances (such as ozone, chlorates, permanganate, hydrogen peroxide) present in a highly oxidizing environment, some nitrites may be oxidized to nitrates, resulting in lower results. The method to eliminate potential interferences is to add reducing agents (such as ascorbic acid). If reducing substances such as sulfides, thiosulfates, ferrous ions, phenols are present, nitrite may be reduced to NO or N2O, resulting in lower results. The elimination method is to add masking agents (such as EDTA masking metal ions) or remove volatile interferences through acidification distillation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric absorption detection of NO2 – by R-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
On account of the color transformation of R-CDs solutions from green to yellow under natural light before and after the addition of the NO2 –, a ratio absorption determination of NO2 – has been constructed below. Fig. 5A displays the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the R-CDs solutions with different concentrations of NO2 –. The absorbance at 350 nm ascends until a new absorption peak appears, and the absorption peak at 410 nm decreases, and the absorption peak at 620 nm basically disappears as the concentration of NO2 – increases (0.00–180.00 μM). Meanwhile, an isoabsorption point emerges at 388 nm. In addition, the absorbance values in the range of 350 nm and 410 nm are roughly from 0.20 to 0.80, so the ratio absorption method could be applied for quantitative analysis of nitrite. The absorbance values at 350 nm and 410 nm have been read from the absorption spectrum, and the A350/A410 (Fig. 5B) has been fitted with an S-type function using the concentration of NO2 – as abscissa, and the equation can be expressed as
y = 0.3592 + 2.1996 ×
0.1106
1 +
10
( 39.7840 - x ) × 0.0819
+
0.8894
1 +

( 67.6778 - x ) × 0.0248
(R2 = 0.9999, n = 19). Meanwhile, there is a linear relationship
y = 0.0257 x - 0.1866
between A350/A410 and the concentration of NO2 – within the concentration range of 40.00–100.00 μM, with the LOD (LOD = 3σ/S) of 0.27 μM. The CIE 1931 Standard Colorimetric System has been employed to confirm the color change of the solution during ultraviolet titration (Fig. 5C). As revealed, the coordinates calculated from (0.282, 0.250) to (0.288, 0.249) is accounted for the apparent migration to red-color regions of the R-CDs while the ambiguous transformation in blue areas, which lays the foundation of smartphone sensing strategy by values of R, G and B. The detection strategy is equipped with eminent sensitivity and a wider detection range than those reported in the literatures (Table S2), which could be promoted for nitrite probes in food security or other scenarios [11,12,54–65].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence and ratio absorption spectroscopy detection of nitrite in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the adaptability of R-CDs sensing means to NO2 – by fluorescence method, three kinds of foods, pickled cucumber, pickled daikon and ham sausage (all purchased from local supermarkets in Taiyuan City, Shanxi Province) have been selected as the research objects. As shown in Table 1 , the NO2 – content in Pickled cucumber juice, Pickled daikon and Ham are 8.49, 11.03 and 8.79 mg/kg according to the established fluorescence analysis method, corresponding to the relative standard deviation (RSD) of 0.55 %, 0.66 % and 0.53 %, respectively. And the NO2 – contents in these samples obtained remain lower than the national food safety content (The content of nitrite in dairy products and meat products shall not exceed 0.20 and 30.00 mg/kg, respectively). Afterwards, the accuracy of the method is evaluated by the spiked recovery experiments. The recoveries keep between 94.70 %∼107.40 % and their RSDs range from 0.36 % to 0.60 %. These measurement data indicate that the established fluorescence analysis method has good precision and accuracy. Analogously, the results of actual samples and labeling recovery for ratio absorption spectroscopy detection by R-CDs are listed as follow (Table 2 ). The contents of NO2 – in Pickled cucumber juice, Pickled daikon and Ham were measured as 8.92, 11.26 and 9.34 mg/kg based on this method, and with the RSD of 0.43 %, 0.57 % and 0.49 %, respectively. The established ratio absorption analysis method has good precision. Similarly, the spiked recovery rate experiments were applied to demonstrate the accuracy of the measurement method. As shown in Table 2, the recovery rate of the added standard is between 95.40 and 104.90 %, This indicates that the data measured by this analysis method has good accuracy. Meanwhile, for the same sample, the data obtained by the established fluorescence analysis method has good comparability with the data obtained by the ratio absorption method. This indicates that the prepared carbon dots also have good application prospects in the detection of nitrate content in actual food samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mobile phone sensing strategy for dual-mode detection of nitrite
ce:section.ce:para: On the basis of the color changes of R-CDs solutions with the addition of different concentrations of NO2 – under the UV light and natural light, photographs of the solutions in the presence of serial concentration gradients of NO2 – have been taken for the construction of convenient and fleet sensing strategy (Fig. 6 A). As manifested in Fig. 6B, the solution of R-CDs (left, 0.00 μM NO2 –) appears grass green under natural light, which transitions to brownish-yellow as the concentration of NO2 – increases (right, 55.00 μM NO2 –). The R, G and B values of the solution are whereafter determined by the color picker application of the smartphone (Huawei P60 Pro), where the R/B ratios are selected to settle the scatter plot with the nitrite concentrations. And the linear equation is R/B = 0.007[NO2 –] + 0.860 (R2 = 0.976, n = 8). Similarly, the R, G and B values are determined by taking photographs of the series of solutions under 365 nm UV lamp (controlling for other conditions unchanged). It is found that with the rises of NO2 – concentration, the fluorescence color becomes from light red to orange to green, and totally quenched ultimately (Fig. 6C). The linear relationship between the B/R value corresponded and NO2 – concentration is deemed to favourable, that the linear equation is set up as B/R = 0.101[NO2 –] + 0.500 (R2 = 0.973, n = 8). The slope in Fig. 6C is larger than that in Fig. 6B, indicating that the fluorescence analysis emerges greater sensitivity than absorption spectroscopy, which is in line with the basic principle of fluorescence spectroscopy.

The experiments of detecting nitrite content and spiked recovery rate in Ham using the established mobile phone sensing mode was shown in Table S3 of the supporting information. The nitrite contents measured in Ham using the mobile phone sensing mode of fluorescence and ratio absorption were 9.82 and 7.89 mg/kg, respectively, which were comparable to the results obtained by the fluorescence and ratio absorption methods on the aforementioned fluorescence and absorption spectrometers. And the RSDs of the two methods were between 8.73 and 15.62 %, indicating that the analysis results have high precision. Moreover, the spiked recovery rates of the two mobile phone sensing modes were between 89.4–113.4 %, implying that the measurement results have high accuracy. Therefore, the established mobile phone sensing mode simplifies the measurement instrument and has high application value in real-time on-site detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Strips-based semi-quantitative detection of nitrite in food
ce:section.ce:para: To design a straightforward method for the analysis of NO2 – by R-CDs, the semi-quantitative test strips have been prepared to better adapt to diversiform practical application scenarios. The quantitative filter paper has been soaked in 37.5 mg∙mL−1 R-CDs solution, and after the solution evenly adhered to the filter paper, it is dried and cut into 1 cm × 1 cm square paper pieces. 1.5 μL of NaNO2 aqueous solutions of multiple concentrations are dropped into the center of the paper, respectively (refer to the figure below for the specific concentration values), and the photographs of the test strip dried are taken. Other conditions maintain unchanged during shooting to avoid interference with detection accuracy by uneven ambient light. It is observed that with the growth of NaNO2 concentration, the color of the test strip transforms from grass green to brownish yellow, whose appearance is analogous to that of the mobile phone sensing platform under visible light (Fig. 6D). Conclusively, the determination strategy is equipped with outstanding detection ability and stability in various media for the determination of nitrite in commercial production or daily life.

In this paper, N, P and S co-doped CDs emitting red fluorescence (R-CDs) are synthesized by the one-step hydrothermal strategy. The optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of R-CDs are 567 nm and 632 nm, respectively, and the relative quantum yield of R-CDs is 0.23. The prepared R-CDs could be applied to the dual-mode (fluorescence and ratiometric absorption spectra) detection of nitrite with the fluorescence detection range from 5.00 to 67.50 µM and LOD of 0.89 µM. Experiments show that NO2 – could statically quench the fluorescence of R-CDs at 632 nm. Subsequently, the detection range of ratio absorption is 10.00–180.00 µM with R2 = 0.9999 and LOD = 0.27 µM. Ultimately, the R-CDs-based smart phone and strip sensing methods are established to achieve simple detection method of nitrite. The experimental results propose that the R-CDs prepared could probe NO2 – in diversiform ways for manifold application scenarios. The cogent application value for actual samples detection, which provides the prospect of promotion and application, definitively.
